Moist northwest flow off Lake Michigan combined with terrain effects brought yet another round of light snow to eastern Kentucky on January 5th. The highest snowfall amount recorded occurred in Letcher county. Two to three inches of snow fell in a 24 hour period, with isolated accumulations of 4-5 inches across the higher terrain. All in all 1-3 inches of snow fell in the affected counties from late in the day on January 4th through late in the morning on the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county EM reports 1-2 inches of new snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snow storm of the 2010-11 winter season dumped between 2 and 12 inches of snow on eastern Kentucky on December 12th and 13th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in Bell, Lee, and Leslie, and McCreary counties where 10-12 inches were reported. There were several culprits for this snow event, as areas of low pressure moving across the lower Great Lakes and up the eastern slopes of the Appalachian Mountains combined forces over the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. The highest snowfall totals occurred along the Kentucky River basin as an intense band of moderate to heavy snowfall remained in place there during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that 3.5 inches of snow had fallenn near Prestonsburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that 15.5 inches of snow had fallen in Wallins Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 24th, Christmas Eve, a low pressure system traversed along the Gulf Of Mexico. Moisture streamed up into the area but the Jackson CWA was spared  the heavier snowfall as that occurred further east. The event was worthy of Winter Weather Advisories with a Winter Storm Warning issued for the higher elevations in Harlan and Letcher counties. A powerful east coast low pressure developed and traveled up the east coast. The fully developed low pressure system brought northerly flow into eastern Kentucky and created an extended upslope event. The event prompted the continuance of the advisories and winter storm warnings into December 27th till noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow had fallen in Mousie since 6 pm yesterday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful area of low pressure moved across the Tennessee valley and then through the mid Atlantic region on January 29th and 30th. The snow first began falling across eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours of January 29th as the storm moved across central Tennessee. The snow finally came to an end late in the day on January 30th as the area of low pressure moved up the east coast. Snowfall amounts of 4-8 inches were quite common around the area, with accumulations as high as 11 inches reported on Black Mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow fell in Whitley City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the 19th and 21st, a storm system stalled over the Mid-Mississippi River Valley before pushing east on the 22nd. Numerous showers and even a few thunderstorms helped to produce average rain amounts around one inch. A few locations received up to two inches. This rainfall pushed the Green River above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Green River. At the river gage at Paradise, the river crested at 383.07 feet on the 25th. Flood stage there is 380 feet. Minor flooding of low-lying woods and fields occurred near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snow storm of the 2010-11 winter season dumped between 2 and 12 inches of snow on eastern Kentucky on December 12th and 13th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in Bell, Lee, and Leslie, and McCreary counties where 10-12 inches were reported. There were several culprits for this snow event, as areas of low pressure moving across the lower Great Lakes and up the eastern slopes of the Appalachian Mountains combined forces over the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. The highest snowfall totals occurred along the Kentucky River basin as an intense band of moderate to heavy snowfall remained in place there during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight tenths of an inch of snow fell near Prestonsburg between 9 pm and midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist northwest flow off Lake Michigan combined with terrain effects brought yet another round of light snow to eastern Kentucky on January 5th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Black ice on bridges and access roads leading into Prestonsburg was the cause of 20 car accidents during the morning of Friday, December 3rd. Five of the accidents resulted in the injuries of 3 people. The most serious accident occurred on the Bert T. Combs bridge coming into Prestonsburg. The bridges and access ramps had to be shut down until the ice was cleared from them later in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 730 am on December 3rd, a total of 20 weather related traffic accidents occurred within Prestonsburg city limits. Five of the 20 accidents resulted in injuries to 3 people. Most of the accidents took place primarily along access roads coming into Prestonsburg. The worst accident occurred on the Bert T. Combs bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snow storm of the 2010-11 winter season dumped between 2 and 12 inches of snow on eastern Kentucky on December 12th and 13th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in Bell, Lee, and Leslie, and McCreary counties where 10-12 inches were reported. There were several culprits for this snow event, as areas of low pressure moving across the lower Great Lakes and up the eastern slopes of the Appalachian Mountains combined forces over the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. The highest snowfall totals occurred along the Kentucky River basin as an intense band of moderate to heavy snowfall remained in place there during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow depth of 3 inches was reported in Fonde. The snow began accumulating around 10 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 24th, Christmas Eve, a low pressure system traversed along the Gulf Of Mexico. Moisture streamed up into the area but the Jackson CWA was spared  the heavier snowfall as that occurred further east. The event was worthy of Winter Weather Advisories with a Winter Storm Warning issued for the higher elevations in Harlan and Letcher counties. A powerful east coast low pressure developed and traveled up the east coast. The fully developed low pressure system brought northerly flow into eastern Kentucky and created an extended upslope event. The event prompted the continuance of the advisories and winter storm warnings into December 27th till noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow had fallen in Booneville since Friday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist northwest flow off Lake Michigan combined with terrain effects brought yet another round of light snow to eastern Kentucky on January 5th. The highest snowfall amount recorded occurred in Letcher county. Two to three inches of snow fell in a 24 hour period, with isolated accumulations of 4-5 inches across the higher terrain. All in all 1-3 inches of snow fell in the affected counties from late in the day on January 4th through late in the morning on the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water plant reports 2 to 3 inches of snow on the ground in Inez with light flurries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and slow moving area of low pressure dumped heavy rainfall on portions of eastern Kentucky on January 24th and 25th. The result for quite a few locations east of I-75 was flooded and washed out roads. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-organized upper level low moved east from the central Plains into the Mid Missouri Valley during the day. Very strong winds aloft accompanied this system. A streak of very strong mid level winds punched northeast from the Arklatex region into the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon. A low level jet from 50 to 60 knots over the Lower Mississippi Valley nosed into the Lower Ohio Valley. At the surface, a low pressure center tracked from north central Arkansas to near St. Louis by late afternoon. Though surface dew points climbed into the lower to mid 60's, extensive cloudcover along and ahead of a warm front limited instability. The instability was generally weak, characterized by cape values below 1000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed branches were breaking off trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 24th, Christmas Eve, a low pressure system traversed along the Gulf Of Mexico. Moisture streamed up into the area but the Jackson CWA was spared  the heavier snowfall as that occurred further east. The event was worthy of Winter Weather Advisories with a Winter Storm Warning issued for the higher elevations in Harlan and Letcher counties. A powerful east coast low pressure developed and traveled up the east coast. The fully developed low pressure system brought northerly flow into eastern Kentucky and created an extended upslope event. The event prompted the continuance of the advisories and winter storm warnings into December 27th till noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half inches of snow fell in Jetts Creek since yesterday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 24th, Christmas Eve, a low pressure system traversed along the Gulf Of Mexico. Moisture streamed up into the area but the Jackson CWA was spared  the heavier snowfall as that occurred further east. The event was worthy of Winter Weather Advisories with a Winter Storm Warning issued for the higher elevations in Harlan and Letcher counties. A powerful east coast low pressure developed and traveled up the east coast. The fully developed low pressure system brought northerly flow into eastern Kentucky and created an extended upslope event. The event prompted the continuance of the advisories and winter storm warnings into December 27th till noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two and a half inches of snow had fallen as of 730 am, with another half inch having fallen since then.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent and slow moving area of low pressure dumped heavy rainfall on portions of eastern Kentucky on January 24th and 25th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was trapped on a flooded road in East Bernstadt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snow storm of the 2010-11 winter season dumped between 2 and 12 inches of snow on eastern Kentucky on December 12th and 13th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in Bell, Lee, and Leslie, and McCreary counties where 10-12 inches were reported. There were several culprits for this snow event, as areas of low pressure moving across the lower Great Lakes and up the eastern slopes of the Appalachian Mountains combined forces over the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. The highest snowfall totals occurred along the Kentucky River basin as an intense band of moderate to heavy snowfall remained in place there during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported that 9.8 inches of snow had fallen in Beattyville, with 3.3 inches of new snow since 7 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and complex storm system brought rain, accumulating snow, and some mixed precipitation to eastern Kentucky from February 8-10th. Several transitions between liquid and wintry precip took place before the precip changed over to all snow by late in the day on February 9th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in the higher terrain along the Virginia and West Virginia borders.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell 6 miles south of Beattyville.

Several transitions between liquid and wintry precip took place before the precip changed over to all snow by late in the day on February 9th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in the higher terrain along the Virginia and West Virginia borders.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and eight tenths of an inch of snow fell in Sandgap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West and northwest flow combined with low and middle level moisture and upslope effects to bring more accumulating snow to eastern Kentucky from late evening on February 16th to overnight hours of February 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three tenths of an inch of new snow fell in Prestonsburg since 730 am. The storm total snowfall since Monday morning was 4.4 inches with a current snow depth of 3 inches. Secondary roads were covered in ice or slush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River continued from late January into the first few days of February. Warmer weather just after the middle of January caused snow in the upper part of the Ohio River basin to melt. This combined with heavy rains around the 20th and 21st of January to send the Ohio River above flood stage. The river peaked during the first few days of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River inundated some fields and several farm roads. At the J.T. Myers Lock and Dam gage near Uniontown, the river crested at 38.91 feet on the 2nd. Flood stage there is 37 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system tracked across the central Appalachians on February 24tha and 25th. The weather system kicked off widespread light snow showers that brought accumulating snowfall to multiple counties across eastern Kentucky. The highest snowfall totals occurred in Harlan and Lecther counties where 5 to 8 inches of snow fell in the higher terrain. One to three inches of snowfall was reported in several other counties around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow fell in Shelbiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought calm winds and clear skies to the eastern Kentucky coalfields during the overnight and early morning hours of April 29th. This synoptic pattern created ideal conditions for widespread frost. Cooperative weather observer reports and various surface observations from April 29th revealed that widespread frost did indeed occur in 19 of the 33 counties in the Jackson weather office forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River continued from late January into the first few days of February. Warmer weather just after the middle of January caused snow in the upper part of the Ohio River basin to melt. This combined with heavy rains around the 20th and 21st of January to send the Ohio River above flood stage. The river peaked during the first few days of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Ohio River inundated some fields and a boat ramp in Paducah. At the Paducah gage, the river crested at 39.25 feet on the 3rd. Flood stage there is 39 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of northern Kentucky received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with slightly lower amounts found further to the east in Lewis county. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven miles south-southeast of Maysville, 3 inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought calm winds and clear skies to the eastern Kentucky coalfields during the overnight and early morning hours of April 29th. This synoptic pattern created ideal conditions for widespread frost. Cooperative weather observer reports and various surface observations from April 29th revealed that widespread frost did indeed occur in 19 of the 33 counties in the Jackson weather office forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was still in control over the area and recent rainfall across eastern Kentucky left enough moisture in place for fog. The fog continued to persist till 9 AM shortly after sunrise. The fog coverage was areas and was dense so a Dense Fog Advisory was issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought lingered across parts of western Kentucky. This drought began during the summer, and a cold and dry December resulted in little improvement. Drought conditions at the end of the month ranged from moderate across the Ohio River counties to severe along the Tennessee border. Subsoil moisture remained low. For the year 2010, most locations ended the year with precipitation deficits of 10 to 13 inches. 2010 was the fourth driest year on record at Paducah, where official records go back to 1949. At Paducah, 2.22 inches of precipitation fell in December. This was 2.16 inches below normal. The long-term moisture deficits were reflected in below normal streamflows on some waterways. Agricultural impacts were minimal since the growing season ended in the fall.

A nearly stationary cold front draped across northern TN, isentropic lift over the boundary, and a moist and unstable air mass were the culprits for severe weather on May 15th across portions of eastern KY. Hail once again was the primary severe weather event reported to the weather office, with hail ranging in size from dimes to golf balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail and a downed tree were reported at Betsey.

A nearly stationary cold front draped across northern TN, isentropic lift over the boundary, and a moist and unstable air mass were the culprits for severe weather on May 15th across portions of eastern KY. Hail once again was the primary severe weather event reported to the weather office, with hail ranging in size from dimes to golf balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail fell at Kerby Knob.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought lingered across parts of western Kentucky. This drought began during the summer, and a cold and dry December resulted in little improvement. Drought conditions at the end of the month ranged from moderate across the Ohio River counties to severe along the Tennessee border. Subsoil moisture remained low. For the year 2010, most locations ended the year with precipitation deficits of 10 to 13 inches. 2010 was the fourth driest year on record at Paducah, where official records go back to 1949. At Paducah, 2.22 inches of precipitation fell in December. This was 2.16 inches below normal. The long-term moisture deficits were reflected in below normal streamflows on some waterways. Agricultural impacts were minimal since the growing season ended in the fall.

High pressure brought calm winds and clear skies to the eastern Kentucky coalfields during the overnight and early morning hours of April 29th. This synoptic pattern created ideal conditions for widespread frost. Cooperative weather observer reports and various surface observations from April 29th revealed that widespread frost did indeed occur in 19 of the 33 counties in the Jackson weather office forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong and severe thunderstorms raked eastern KY during the afternoon and evening hours of June 21st. The majority of the damage and flooding caused by the storms occurred north of the Hal Rogers Parkway and Highway 80 Corridor. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received at the Jackson weather office, along with a few reports of subsevere hail and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Grassy Knoll Road just off of Martha Mills Road near Tilton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stratiform area of rain and embedded thunderstorms occurred north of an area of intense convection over northwest Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bolt of lightning struck a large oak tree over a hundred years old. The tree crashed onto three vehicles parked in a driveway. A full-size pickup truck was totalled, another older truck was extensively damaged, and a sports car was heavily damaged. Phone lines were taken down by the tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was still in control over the area and recent rainfall across eastern Kentucky left enough moisture in place for fog. The fog continued to persist till 9 AM shortly after sunrise. The fog coverage was areas and was dense so a Dense Fog Advisory was issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought lingered across parts of western Kentucky. This drought began during the summer, and a cold and dry December resulted in little improvement. Drought conditions at the end of the month ranged from moderate across the Ohio River counties to severe along the Tennessee border. Subsoil moisture remained low. For the year 2010, most locations ended the year with precipitation deficits of 10 to 13 inches. 2010 was the fourth driest year on record at Paducah, where official records go back to 1949. At Paducah, 2.22 inches of precipitation fell in December. This was 2.16 inches below normal. The long-term moisture deficits were reflected in below normal streamflows on some waterways. Agricultural impacts were minimal since the growing season ended in the fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of northern Kentucky received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with slightly lower amounts found further to the east in Lewis county. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three miles southwest of Morning View, a spotter measured 3.4 inches of snow. Another located one mile northeast of Park Hills measured 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought lingered across parts of western Kentucky. This drought began during the summer, and a cold and dry December resulted in little improvement. Drought conditions at the end of the month ranged from moderate across the Ohio River counties to severe along the Tennessee border. Subsoil moisture remained low. For the year 2010, most locations ended the year with precipitation deficits of 10 to 13 inches. 2010 was the fourth driest year on record at Paducah, where official records go back to 1949. At Paducah, 2.22 inches of precipitation fell in December. This was 2.16 inches below normal. The long-term moisture deficits were reflected in below normal streamflows on some waterways. Agricultural impacts were minimal since the growing season ended in the fall.

High pressure brought calm winds and clear skies to the eastern Kentucky coalfields during the overnight and early morning hours of April 28th. This synoptic pattern created ideal conditions for widespread frost. Cooperative weather observer reports and various surface observations from April 28th revealed that widespread frost did indeed occur in 31 of the 33 counties in the Jackson weather office forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary cold front draped across northern TN, isentropic lift over the boundary, and a moist and unstable air mass were the culprits for severe weather on May 15th across portions of eastern KY. Hail once again was the primary severe weather event reported to the weather office, with hail ranging in size from dimes to golf balls.

Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of northern Kentucky received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with slightly lower amounts found further to the east in Lewis county. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In both Napoleon and 4 miles north-northeast of Glencoe, 2.5 inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved across Tennessee on Thursday. Moisture moving north ahead of a surface trough spread a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across all of central Kentucky during the late evening hours on December 15th. Across south central Kentucky, freezing rain changed to all rain with only light icing. Farther north, generally northeast of a line from Meade to Casey Counties, light sleet turned into freezing rain. Ice accumulated from around one quarter of an inch to one third of an inch before ending around dawn on the 16th. Snow fell across the northern Bluegrass Region northeast of a line from Trimble to Clark Counties. Snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches across this area before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain brought between one quarter and one third of an inch of ice around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front from an occluded low over the Great Lakes was swinging southward toward central Kentucky during the afternoon hours of May 21. A shortwave progressing across the region helped to spawn strong thunderstorms. With ample shear one of these storms was able to produce a tornado that touched down multiple times across Breckinridge and Hardin counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey and interviews with residents confirmed a tornado touched down at approximately 828 PM EDT just west of Big Springs.  A large barn suffered significant roof and wall damage resulting in the tornado to be rated EF-1 here with 85-90 mph winds.  Additional damage to hardwood trees was noted along Highway 333 south.  The tornado appeared to be skipping along the path at this time.  The tornado lifted above the community of Big Springs, then touched down again along High Plains Road before lifting again.  It then touched down again along Rineyville-Big Springs road in Harding county where it damaged a double wide mobile home.  The damage at this location was consistent with EF-0 tornado damage with winds of 80-85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 2nd, a large area of low pressure tracked across the southern United States. A combination of cold air from Canada being drawn into the circulation of this system from the north, and lift associated with the low itself and upslope affects, led to the formation of scattered snow showers across portions of eastern Kentucky. The areas that received accumulating snowfall were in Knott, Harlan and Letcher counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell in Pound Gap near Jenkins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough moving across Tennessee combined with an upper low sliding south across the upper Midwest brought a mixture of heavy snow, sleet and rain across central Kentucky Tuesday morning February 9th. Snow began during the evening hours across south central Kentucky and moved north of Interstate 64 by midnight. By the early morning hours, snow had turned to sleet and rain south and east of a line from Breckinridge County through Henry County. Along the Ohio river, banded precipitation brought intermittent bursts of heavy snow around 8 to 9 am. The heaviest snow totals fell along the Ohio River, where 4 to 7 inches of accumulation were common. Sleet and rain limited snowfall amounts  to 1 to 3 inches across south central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 6 inches of snow fell countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving through the southeastern states and then off the eastern seaboard combined with an upper level disturbance to bring wintry weather to eastern Kentucky on the morning of February 2nd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a trace of snowfall and a light glaze of ice between 2 and 6 am near Prestonsburg. Less than a tenth of an inch of ice was reported, but it was enough to form a glaze of ice on untreated and exposed surfaces with isolated patches of ice on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving through the southeastern states and then off the eastern seaboard combined with an upper level disturbance to bring wintry weather to eastern Kentucky on the morning of February 2nd. Some light accumulations of ice and snow were reported across several counties. The icy conditions led to a serious car accident on Highway 23 in Thornton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported one tenth of an inch of new ice accumulation in Smilax. The ice was accumulating on walkways, roadways, power lines and trees in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and complex storm system brought rain, accumulating snow, and some mixed precipitation to eastern Kentucky from February 8-10th. Several transitions between liquid and wintry precip took place before the precip changed over to all snow by late in the day on February 9th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in the higher terrain along the Virginia and West Virginia borders.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported that 2.75 inches of snow had fallen since last night. Based on radar loops and surface observations, the snow likely fell in two seperate events, one between 8 and 10 pm last night and the other since about 7 am this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some light wintry precipitation fell from a deck of low level cloud cover during the late evening hours on February 23rd. A single report of freezing rain and minor ice accumulations was received from an emergency manager in Elliott county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain left a very light glaze of ice on exposed surfaces in Sandy Hook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow and shallow low level moisture led to the occurrence of light rain, drizzle, and some isolated patches of freezing drizzle in the higher elevations along the Virginia border on February 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Weather Service employee reported that a mixture of snow and freezing drizzle was falling in the higher elevations of Letcher county just across the Virginia line from Wise county. A light glaze of ice was accumulating on exposed objects.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Mississippi Valley strengthened as it moved into the Ohio Valley early on Tuesday, February 9th. Snow began on Monday night and spread northeast early on Tuesday. Later in the afternoon, this snow mixed with rain south of the Ohio River. As the system pulled out of the region Tuesday night, colder air on strong winds changed the precipitation back to snow, with additional accumulations noted in blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Falmouth measured 3.5 inches of snow early in the morning. The emergency manager reported 5.5 inches of snow later in the day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the past hour 0.10 inches of snow fell, with a total accumulation of snow and slush of 0.60 inches. A trace of freezing rain also fell, leaving a light glaze of ice on all exposed surfaces. Roads in the area were covered in snow, frozen slush, a light glaze of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough moving across Tennessee combined with an upper low sliding south across the upper Midwest brought a mixture of heavy snow, sleet and rain across central Kentucky Tuesday morning February 9th. Snow began during the evening hours across south central Kentucky and moved north of Interstate 64 by midnight. By the early morning hours, snow had turned to sleet and rain south and east of a line from Breckinridge County through Henry County. Along the Ohio river, banded precipitation brought intermittent bursts of heavy snow around 8 to 9 am. The heaviest snow totals fell along the Ohio River, where 4 to 7 inches of accumulation were common. Sleet and rain limited snowfall amounts  to 1 to 3 inches across south central Kentucky and the Bluegrass Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 5 inches of snow fell countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-organized upper level low moved east from the central Plains into the Mid Missouri Valley during the day. Very strong winds aloft accompanied this system. A streak of very strong mid level winds punched northeast from the Arklatex region into the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon. A low level jet from 50 to 60 knots over the Lower Mississippi Valley nosed into the Lower Ohio Valley. At the surface, a low pressure center tracked from north central Arkansas to near St. Louis by late afternoon. Though surface dew points climbed into the lower to mid 60's, extensive cloudcover along and ahead of a warm front limited instability. The instability was generally weak, characterized by cape values below 1000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three-quarters of an inch of rain fell in 14 minutes.

On March 2nd, a large area of low pressure tracked across the southern United States. A combination of cold air from Canada being drawn into the circulation of this system from the north, and lift associated with the low itself and upslope affects, led to the formation of scattered snow showers across portions of eastern Kentucky. The areas that received accumulating snowfall were in Knott, Harlan and Letcher counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Dept of Transportation reported that two inches of snow fell in Hindman in the higher elevations.

A cold front trailed south-southwestward from a surface low pressure center over west central Illinois through southeast Missouri. This cold front provided the low level lift for short lines of thunderstorms to develop during the late afternoon. Some breaks in the clouds allowed sunshine to destabilize the atmosphere, but mixed layer capes remained at or below 1000 j/kg due to modest lapse rates and low-level moisture. Surface dew points around 60 degrees pooled along the front. Wind profiles were unidirectional and generally parallel to the front. As a result, the storms were mainly organized into short linear structures with some bowing segments that produced damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dime-size hail was accompanied by 40 to 50 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-organized upper level low moved east from the central Plains into the Mid Missouri Valley during the day. Very strong winds aloft accompanied this system. A streak of very strong mid level winds punched northeast from the Arklatex region into the Lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon. A low level jet from 50 to 60 knots over the Lower Mississippi Valley nosed into the Lower Ohio Valley. At the surface, a low pressure center tracked from north central Arkansas to near St. Louis by late afternoon. Though surface dew points climbed into the lower to mid 60's, extensive cloudcover along and ahead of a warm front limited instability. The instability was generally weak, characterized by cape values below 1000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees or tree limbs were blown down. Minor damage occurred to a home, consisting mainly of gutter damage. The tornado track was mostly across fields. The tornado was photographed near the initial touchdown and was also observed less than one mile from Pembroke. Peak winds were estimated near 75 mph.

Severe thunderstorms brought flooding and tree damage, building, and powerline damage to portions of eastern Kentucky on September 11, 2010. The worst of the flooding actually occurred in the normally flood resistent Pulaski county. As many as five inches of rain fell in the Somerset area with numerous streets flooded due to the heavy rainfall. Numerous trees were blown down in near the Wayne county hospital in Monticello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and onto a home in Trosper off of KY Hwy 225.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure, both at the surface and aloft, brought record breaking heat to eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of August 4th. High temperatures across the area were between 95 and 100 degrees for numerous locations. All 17 counties in the Excessive Heat Warning for that day met or exceeded warning criteria. In fact, the only county of the 33 counties covered by the Jackson weather office that did not meet heat warning criteria on August 4th was Letcher. Heat indices ran between 105 and 115, with a few areas approaching 120 degrees, on this day. The summer of 2010 will likely rank as one of if not the hottest summer on record in eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat warning criteria were met with highs between 95 and 100 and heat indices between 105 and 115.

Convection never organized into complete lines, but some individual cells became strong to severe. Moisture had advected northward into the area in advance of the front. Motion of the storms was fairly quick and towards the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved across Tennessee on Thursday. Moisture moving north ahead of a surface trough spread a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across all of central Kentucky during the late evening hours on December 15th. Across south central Kentucky, freezing rain changed to all rain with only light icing. Farther north, generally northeast of a line from Meade to Casey Counties, light sleet turned into freezing rain. Ice accumulated from around one quarter of an inch to one third of an inch before ending around dawn on the 16th. Snow fell across the northern Bluegrass Region northeast of a line from Trimble to Clark Counties. Snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches across this area before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations ranged from one quarter to one third of an inch countywide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain during the early morning hours brought ice accumulations of between one quarter to one third of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level impulse on the northern periphery of a ridge resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the evening. A large complex of thunderstorms over Indiana weakened as it pushed into Ohio and Kentucky, but still managed to produce a strong outflow boundary which raced out ahead of the storms. The strongest winds occurred where the outflow rushed out fastest, despite a lack of high velocity and sometimes a lack of storms on radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

Farther north, generally northeast of a line from Meade to Casey Counties, light sleet turned into freezing rain. Ice accumulated from around one quarter of an inch to one third of an inch before ending around dawn on the 16th. Snow fell across the northern Bluegrass Region northeast of a line from Trimble to Clark Counties. Snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches across this area before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow left minor accumulations before changing to freezing rain. One quarter of an inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought continued across much of western Kentucky during the month of November. Widespread heavy rainfall on the 24th and 25th resulted in some improvement in drought conditions. Following this heavy rainfall, drought levels improved from severe to moderate along and west of a line from Henderson to Madisonville. Places south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville remained in severe drought. Monthly rainfall totals were near normal or above normal in most areas. However, the year-to-date rainfall deficit was still to 10 to 11 inches. Topsoil moisture was replenished, but subsoil moisture remained low. Due to high fire danger, bans on outdoor burning were in effect for numerous counties in western Kentucky at the start of the month. Most burn bans were lifted after heavy rainfall late in the month. Crop harvests were completed in October, but final crop damage figures were not yet available. The cumulative effect of this drought, a catastrophic ice storm in '09, winds from Hurricane Ike in '08, and a record late spring freeze in '07 resulted in a mortality spiral among trees and shrubs. According to a local arborist, the series of damaging weather events diminished the long-term ability of trees to recover from future events.

A large brush fire consumed 234 acres in the Lyon County portion of the Land Between The Lakes National Recreation Area. The fire was just off the east side of Kentucky Highway 453, known as The Trace. Conditions were ripe for such a fire due to the summer and fall drought. Very low relative humidity on consecutive afternoons dried fine fuels such as leaves and grass. Green vegetation that normally slows the rate of fire spread in the summer was dormant due to killing frosts in October.

Strong south winds developed ahead of a cold front trailing south from an unseasonably strong low pressure system over the Upper Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts were from 40 to 50 mph. Some tree limbs were blown down, taking power lines with them. Scattered power outages were reported in Marshall County.

On October 26 2010, a very strong cold front with strong winds moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys. A strong low level jet of 50 to 60 knots developed at 850 MB allowing for a fair amount of shear that prompted a tornado watch. At 13Z the main front and associated squall line was over Western Kentucky just west of I-65. A tornado watch for eastern Kentucky was first issued at 13z. The squall line first reached the Jackson county warning area border around 130PM. The main threat was the possibility of isolated tornadoes with severe criteria wind being the most likely occurrence. The first severe weather warning was issued at 131 PM for Fleming County and warnings continued into the evening. Tornado warnings were issued as well and the first one as at 315PM in Wayne county as some storms took on super cell characteristics. The event ended at 8PM when the Tornado Watch was allowed to expire. The last short fused product was issued at 453pm and was a tornado warning which was the last warning addressing severe characteristics. Storms continued that were worthy of an SPS until 6pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Inez.

Clear skies and calm winds set the stage for a widespread hard freeze during the overnight and early morning hours of October 30th. Temperatures across all of eastern Kentucky dropped to at or below freezing. The valleys had the coldest temperatures as lows is those locations dropped into the mid 20s. This freeze effectively ended the 2010 growing season across eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread hard freeze occurred.

A strong cold front moved through the region during the day on October 26.  During the early morning hours a low level jet of 60 to 70 knots formed.  A squall line developed ahead of the front with produced widespread damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.  Additionally a few tornadoes formed along this squall line as it moved across central Kentucky.  Tree damage, damage to buildings, and power lines down were all reported with this squall line.  Ahead of this squall line the tight pressure gradient led to winds gusting up to 50 mph preceding the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey confirmed an EF-0 tornado from 2.2 miles SW of Milton to 1.1 miles SW of Milton.  The path length was 1.1 miles with a maximum path width of 20 yards.  Maximum sustained winds were 70 mph.  In addition to tree and fence damage, a front porch roof was destroyed on one home, along with other roof and garage damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first blast of winter weather followed a strong cold front that swept rapidly east across the Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valleys. Behind the front, widespread snow showers and very windy conditions produced difficult travel conditions. Northwest winds ranged from 20 to 30 mph with higher gusts. Snow accumulated from one to three inches across western Kentucky. The lowest amounts were an inch or less near the Mississippi River, including the cities of Bardwell and Hickman. Widespread amounts from 2 to 3 inches covered the remainder of western Kentucky. The snow came down rather heavily at times for a couple hours, reducing visibility to around one-half mile. Numerous accidents were reported around the region, including an overturned vehicle on the Interstate 24 bridge over the Tennessee River. Strong winds caused blowing and drifting that produced slippery spots well after the snow ended.

Numerous accidents were reported around the region, including an overturned vehicle on the Interstate 24 bridge over the Tennessee River. Strong winds caused blowing and drifting that produced slippery spots well after the snow ended.

Strong south winds developed ahead of a cold front trailing south from an unseasonably strong low pressure system over the Upper Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts were from 40 to 50 mph. Some tree limbs were blown down, taking power lines with them. Scattered power outages were reported in Marshall County.

On October 26 2010, a very strong cold front with strong winds moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys. A strong low level jet of 50 to 60 knots developed at 850 MB allowing for a fair amount of shear that prompted a tornado watch. At 13Z the main front and associated squall line was over Western Kentucky just west of I-65. A tornado watch for eastern Kentucky was first issued at 13z. The squall line first reached the Jackson county warning area border around 130PM. The main threat was the possibility of isolated tornadoes with severe criteria wind being the most likely occurrence. The first severe weather warning was issued at 131 PM for Fleming County and warnings continued into the evening. Tornado warnings were issued as well and the first one as at 315PM in Wayne county as some storms took on super cell characteristics. The event ended at 8PM when the Tornado Watch was allowed to expire. The last short fused product was issued at 453pm and was a tornado warning which was the last warning addressing severe characteristics. Storms continued that were worthy of an SPS until 6pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust estimated at 50 mph occurred in Somerset.

Severe drought continued across much of western Kentucky during the month of November. Widespread heavy rainfall on the 24th and 25th resulted in some improvement in drought conditions. Following this heavy rainfall, drought levels improved from severe to moderate along and west of a line from Henderson to Madisonville. Places south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville remained in severe drought. Monthly rainfall totals were near normal or above normal in most areas. However, the year-to-date rainfall deficit was still to 10 to 11 inches. Topsoil moisture was replenished, but subsoil moisture remained low. Due to high fire danger, bans on outdoor burning were in effect for numerous counties in western Kentucky at the start of the month. Most burn bans were lifted after heavy rainfall late in the month. Crop harvests were completed in October, but final crop damage figures were not yet available. The cumulative effect of this drought, a catastrophic ice storm in '09, winds from Hurricane Ike in '08, and a record late spring freeze in '07 resulted in a mortality spiral among trees and shrubs. According to a local arborist, the series of damaging weather events diminished the long-term ability of trees to recover from future events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe thunderstorms was triggered by an unseasonably strong low pressure system over the Upper Mississippi Valley. The trailing cold front swept east across western Kentucky. Strong south winds ahead of this front brought adequate moisture and instability for a line of low-topped convection. Minimal lightning activity was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few limbs and small trees were blown down.

A strong cold front moved through the region during the day on October 26.  During the early morning hours a low level jet of 60 to 70 knots formed.  A squall line developed ahead of the front with produced widespread damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.  Additionally a few tornadoes formed along this squall line as it moved across central Kentucky.  Tree damage, damage to buildings, and power lines down were all reported with this squall line.  Ahead of this squall line the tight pressure gradient led to winds gusting up to 50 mph preceding the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team confirmed an EF-1 tornado in Warren county.  The tornado touched down 3.2 miles southeast of Bowling Green at 11:08 am CDT and lifted 4.5 miles east-southeast of Bowling Green at 11:11 am CDT.  Peak winds were around 90 mph with a path width of 60 yards.  A tree was uprooted at the beginning of the path with a home damaged along Cemetery Rd.  The tornado intensified as it progressed northeast, with a barn destroyed near the end of its track.

Widespread amounts from 2 to 3 inches covered the remainder of western Kentucky. The snow came down rather heavily at times for a couple hours, reducing visibility to around one-half mile. Numerous accidents were reported around the region, including an overturned vehicle on the Interstate 24 bridge over the Tennessee River. Strong winds caused blowing and drifting that produced slippery spots well after the snow ended.

The first blast of winter weather followed a strong cold front that swept rapidly east across the Mississippi and Lower Ohio Valleys. Behind the front, widespread snow showers and very windy conditions produced difficult travel conditions. Northwest winds ranged from 20 to 30 mph with higher gusts. Widespread amounts from 2 to 3 inches covered the remainder of western Kentucky. The snow came down rather heavily at times for a couple hours, reducing visibility to around one-half mile. Numerous accidents were reported around the region, including an overturned vehicle on the Interstate 24 bridge over the Tennessee River. Strong winds caused blowing and drifting that produced slippery spots well after the snow ended.

Severe drought continued across much of western Kentucky during the month of November. Widespread heavy rainfall on the 24th and 25th resulted in some improvement in drought conditions. Following this heavy rainfall, drought levels improved from severe to moderate along and west of a line from Henderson to Madisonville. Places south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville remained in severe drought. Monthly rainfall totals were near normal or above normal in most areas. However, the year-to-date rainfall deficit was still to 10 to 11 inches. Topsoil moisture was replenished, but subsoil moisture remained low. Due to high fire danger, bans on outdoor burning were in effect for numerous counties in western Kentucky at the start of the month. Most burn bans were lifted after heavy rainfall late in the month. Crop harvests were completed in October, but final crop damage figures were not yet available. The cumulative effect of this drought, a catastrophic ice storm in '09, winds from Hurricane Ike in '08, and a record late spring freeze in '07 resulted in a mortality spiral among trees and shrubs. According to a local arborist, the series of damaging weather events diminished the long-term ability of trees to recover from future events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and calm winds set the stage for a widespread hard freeze during the overnight and early morning hours of October 30th. This freeze effectively ended the 2010 growing season across eastern Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 16th, a low pressure system developed in the plains and then tracked east across the Tennessee Valley. The warm front moved just south of the forecast area. Wintry precipitation fell as freezing rain from the Tennessee border and north up to the Mountain Parkway. The northern counties such as Fleming, Bath, and Rowan received all snow from the event. South of the Mountain Parkway, heavy ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch were common. This resulted in many homes being without power for an extended period of time. Perry county had more than 2000 residents without power. Breathitt county had 200 customers without power. Leslie county had nearly 4000 customers without power. The northern counties reported 4 to 6 inches of snow from the storm. The surface still had a persistent cold layer and freezing drizzle continued into the 17th. This prompted the issuance of a new winter weather advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch and a half of snow fell in Fleming-Neon. The snow changed over to freezing rain around 540 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 24th, Christmas Eve, a low pressure system traversed along the Gulf Of Mexico. Moisture streamed up into the area but the Jackson CWA was spared  the heavier snowfall as that occurred further east. The event was worthy of Winter Weather Advisories with a Winter Storm Warning issued for the higher elevations in Harlan and Letcher counties. A powerful east coast low pressure developed and traveled up the east coast. The fully developed low pressure system brought northerly flow into eastern Kentucky and created an extended upslope event. The event prompted the continuance of the advisories and winter storm warnings into December 27th till noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch of snow has fallen in the past 12 hours 2 miles south of Shelbiana. The snow was blowing and drifting.

Perry county had more than 2000 residents without power. Breathitt county had 200 customers without power. Leslie county had nearly 4000 customers without power. The northern counties reported 4 to 6 inches of snow from the storm. The surface still had a persistent cold layer and freezing drizzle continued into the 17th. This prompted the issuance of a new winter weather advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The State Police reported that the roads in Whitesburg were covered with freezing rain and snow. A semi truck jack knifed on US 23. Very hazardous road conditions existed countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 24th, Christmas Eve, a low pressure system traversed along the Gulf Of Mexico. Moisture streamed up into the area but the Jackson CWA was spared  the heavier snowfall as that occurred further east. The event was worthy of Winter Weather Advisories with a Winter Storm Warning issued for the higher elevations in Harlan and Letcher counties. A powerful east coast low pressure developed and traveled up the east coast. The fully developed low pressure system brought northerly flow into eastern Kentucky and created an extended upslope event. The event prompted the continuance of the advisories and winter storm warnings into December 27th till noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The running snowfall total in Relief was 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms raced eastward across Illinois and Indiana at 45 mph. The trailing southern end of the bow crossed parts of northwest Kentucky, accompanied by locally damaging winds. The line of storms may have been initiated by a weak upper level disturbance entering northern Illinois. As it moved east, the line gained strength as it fed on a very moist and unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 65 mph was measured. A tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms brought flooding to an area of eastern KY south of the Hal Rogers Parkway and Highway corridor on August 18th. Numerous reports of flooding were received from Whitley and Bell counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Corbin Police Dept reported that parts of Bacon Street near Corbin were still under water. Most of the flood waters in Corbin had already receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked eastward across western Tennessee. A warm front lifted northward ahead of this system, causing freezing rain. In the Henderson and Owensboro areas, around one-quarter inch of glaze formed on most surfaces, including trees and power lines. The main impact was on travel. Roads became hazardous due to ice and slush. There was slightly less precipitation south and west of the Henderson and Owensboro areas, where less than one-quarter inch of glaze formed. The duration and impact of the ice diminished toward the south. Along and south of a Paducah to Madisonville line, the freezing rain resulted in just a few minor accidents.

On Saturday December the 11th, a strong low pressure system pushed through eastern Kentucky. A sharp transition after frontal and a prolonged up slope event took place after frontal passage. The amount of moisture wrapped around the low and the strong northwest flow prompted an issuance of winter storm warnings in the the eastern mountain counties where 6 to 12 inches were expected in the mountains and 4 to 6 were expected in the lower elevations. A winter weather advisory was issued for the other counties with 3 to 6 inches expected. The event began around 7 pm Sunday and the heavier snow began around 11 pm Sunday and lasted till about 8 am Monday morning. The event ended by 9 pm Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm total was 6 inches with a snow depth of 5 inches at 8 am on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought continued across much of western Kentucky during the month of November. Widespread heavy rainfall on the 24th and 25th resulted in some improvement in drought conditions. Following this heavy rainfall, drought levels improved from severe to moderate along and west of a line from Henderson to Madisonville. Places south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville remained in severe drought. Monthly rainfall totals were near normal or above normal in most areas. However, the year-to-date rainfall deficit was still to 10 to 11 inches. Topsoil moisture was replenished, but subsoil moisture remained low. Due to high fire danger, bans on outdoor burning were in effect for numerous counties in western Kentucky at the start of the month. Most burn bans were lifted after heavy rainfall late in the month. Crop harvests were completed in October, but final crop damage figures were not yet available. The cumulative effect of this drought, a catastrophic ice storm in '09, winds from Hurricane Ike in '08, and a record late spring freeze in '07 resulted in a mortality spiral among trees and shrubs. According to a local arborist, the series of damaging weather events diminished the long-term ability of trees to recover from future events.

In the Henderson and Owensboro areas, around one-quarter inch of glaze formed on most surfaces, including trees and power lines. The main impact was on travel. Roads became hazardous due to ice and slush. There was slightly less precipitation south and west of the Henderson and Owensboro areas, where less than one-quarter inch of glaze formed. The duration and impact of the ice diminished toward the south. Along and south of a Paducah to Madisonville line, the freezing rain resulted in just a few minor accidents.

On Saturday December the 11th, a strong low pressure system pushed through eastern Kentucky. A sharp transition after frontal and a prolonged up slope event took place after frontal passage. The amount of moisture wrapped around the low and the strong northwest flow prompted an issuance of winter storm warnings in the the eastern mountain counties where 6 to 12 inches were expected in the mountains and 4 to 6 were expected in the lower elevations. A winter weather advisory was issued for the other counties with 3 to 6 inches expected. The event began around 7 pm Sunday and the heavier snow began around 11 pm Sunday and lasted till about 8 am Monday morning. The event ended by 9 pm Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 12 hour snowfall total was 6 inches ending at 630 PM.

Severe drought continued across much of western Kentucky during the month of November. Widespread heavy rainfall on the 24th and 25th resulted in some improvement in drought conditions. Following this heavy rainfall, drought levels improved from severe to moderate along and west of a line from Henderson to Madisonville. Places south of a line from Paducah to Madisonville remained in severe drought. Monthly rainfall totals were near normal or above normal in most areas. However, the year-to-date rainfall deficit was still to 10 to 11 inches. Topsoil moisture was replenished, but subsoil moisture remained low. Due to high fire danger, bans on outdoor burning were in effect for numerous counties in western Kentucky at the start of the month. Most burn bans were lifted after heavy rainfall late in the month. Crop harvests were completed in October, but final crop damage figures were not yet available. The cumulative effect of this drought, a catastrophic ice storm in '09, winds from Hurricane Ike in '08, and a record late spring freeze in '07 resulted in a mortality spiral among trees and shrubs. According to a local arborist, the series of damaging weather events diminished the long-term ability of trees to recover from future events.

A large area of high pressure, both at the surface and aloft, brought record breaking heat to eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours of August 4th. High temperatures across the area were between 95 and 100 degrees for numerous locations. All 17 counties in the Excessive Heat Warning for that day met or exceeded warning criteria. In fact, the only county of the 33 counties covered by the Jackson weather office that did not meet heat warning criteria on August 4th was Letcher. Heat indices ran between 105 and 115, with a few areas approaching 120 degrees, on this day. The summer of 2010 will likely rank as one of if not the hottest summer on record in eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat warning criteria were met with highs between 95 and 100 and heat indices between 105 and 115.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was primed for strong to severe thunderstorm activity on August 5th. At the surface, a very warm and moist air mass was in place, as evidenced by the 70+ degree dewpoints that were being seen in the surface obs and the -4 to -6 lifted indices that were showing up in the SPC forecast maps. A 25-40 kt low level jet and a bullseye of moisture convergent were also present. The storms began firing along a cold front that was approaching from the northwest. The most widespread activity took place during the afternoon hours. All of the damage reports received that day were of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on KY Hwy 1258.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets in the city of Corbin were still experiencing flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms brought flooding to an area of eastern KY south of the Hal Rogers Parkway and Highway corridor on August 18th. Numerous reports of flooding were received from Whitley and Bell counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Corbin police dept reported that flooding was still occurring throughout Corbin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines and complexes of thunderstorms formed to the east of a weak surface trough, and east of a stationary frontal boundary that stretched from the lower Great Lakes to the Mid Atlantic coast. A few of these storms caused isolated tree damage and heavy rainfall at several locations across eastern Kentucky on August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at Hwy 409 in Bruin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was primed for strong to severe thunderstorm activity on August 5th. At the surface, a very warm and moist air mass was in place, as evidenced by the 70+ degree dewpoints that were being seen in the surface obs and the -4 to -6 lifted indices that were showing up in the SPC forecast maps. A 25-40 kt low level jet and a bullseye of moisture convergent were also present. The storms began firing along a cold front that was approaching from the northwest. The most widespread activity took place during the afternoon hours. All of the damage reports received that day were of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along KY Hwy 194.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown onto Hwy 26 at Eaton, completely blocking the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local dispatch office reported that three feet of water was across the intersection of US Hwy 25W and KY Hwy 204. Both roads were closed prior to this time due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable air mass, combined with a strong cold front, set the stage for severe weather across eastern Kentucky on August 14th. Strong to severe thunderstorms downed trees and power lines, caused flooding, and did damage to a few homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported in and around Somerset including along Hwy 27. Crews were still out at report issuance restoring power to residences and businesses around Somerset.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county dispatch office reported 3 to 4 feet of water over Welchburg Rd in Tyner. Heavy rain began falling around 920 pm. The water was still over the road as of 11 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same ridge of high pressure that brought oppressive heat and humidity to eastern Kentucky most of the week, again brought scorching heat to the coalfields on August 13th. High temperatures were in the low to mid 90s with heat indices once again around 100 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the low to mid 90s with heat indices once again around 100 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in the upper atmosphere, a surface boundary, arctic air, and abundant low and middle level moisture all combined to bring a prolonged period of light accumulating snow to eastern Kentucky on January 7th. The snow began during the midmorning hours on January 7th and lasted until 7 or 8z on January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and six tenths of an inch of new snow fell in Clay City between 11 am and 5 pm. The total snow depth was 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although light snow did fall off and on on January 8th, most of the snowfall reports received at the Jackson weather office were 24 hour totals. The bulk of the snow that fell across eastern Kentucky occurred from late morning on January 7th through 2 or 3 am on January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported 0.6 inches of new snow over the past 6 hours near Talbert and still snowing pretty hard. There was a total snow depth of 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was about 1 inch of snow covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a compact, intense low center over southeast Missouri. The air mass became only marginally unstable as a mid-level cold pocket steepened lapse rates, and surface heating eroded the cap. Low-topped thunderstorms producing heavy rain and small hail tracked along a warm front lifting into far southwest Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lanes of U.S. Highway 60 in the city of Henderson were blocked due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance sliding southeast across Illinois combined with weak low pressure across Kentucky to bring snow across the northern Kentucky Bluegrass. Light snow developed during the early morning hours and continued intermittently through the evening hours on the 4th of December. A band of moderate snow developed shortly after dawn and brought 2 to 5 inches of snow along a thin arc stretching from Trimble County through Clark and Madison Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 5 inches of snow fell near Frankfort, Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved across Tennessee on Thursday. Moisture moving north ahead of a surface trough spread a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across all of central Kentucky during the late evening hours on December 15th. Across south central Kentucky, freezing rain changed to all rain with only light icing. Farther north, generally northeast of a line from Meade to Casey Counties, light sleet turned into freezing rain. Ice accumulated from around one quarter of an inch to one third of an inch before ending around dawn on the 16th. Snow fell across the northern Bluegrass Region northeast of a line from Trimble to Clark Counties. Snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches across this area before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain brought ice accumulations of between one quarter to one third of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent northwest flow along with meager low and middle level moisture led to the occurrence of scattered light snow showers across eastern Kentucky on January 11th and 12th. The snow first began accumulating late during the night of January 11th and finally stopped accumulating during the late afternoon of January 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three and two tenths of an inch of snow had accumulated since Thursday night. An eight of an inch of ice due to freezing rain had also accumulated overnight in Thousandsticks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the southern plains eastward through the Tennessee Valley on Thursday, December 16th. A significant amount of moist air was pulled into the Ohio Valley ahead of this surface low, diminishing significantly as it went further north past the Ohio River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was measured in Vanceburg, with 3.3 inches measured a few hours earlier in Tollesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow was on the ground in Morehead and it was still snowing hard at the time the report was received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county sheriff's office reported 1 to 2 inches of snow the ground in Mount Sterling with light snow still falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county highway garage reported about 1 inch of snow on the ground in Frenchburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 3 inches of snow on the ground in Phelps with snow still falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was about 1 inch of snow on the ground in Hindman with light flurries falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow on the ground in Hazard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow on the ground around Hazard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and two tenths of an inch of snow were measured by an NWS employee 2 miles east southeast of Talbert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid temperatures combined with upslope flow over eastern Kentucky terrain, and a wave of energy aloft brought widespread snow showers to eastern Kentucky on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was about 1 inch of snow on the ground with snow ongoing 2 miles east of Ravenna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a compact, intense low center over southeast Missouri. The air mass became only marginally unstable as a mid-level cold pocket steepened lapse rates, and surface heating eroded the cap. Low-topped thunderstorms producing heavy rain and small hail tracked along a warm front lifting into far southwest Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred for about one minute along U.S. Highway 641.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of northern Kentucky received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with slightly lower amounts found further to the east in Lewis county. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three miles west of Union, a spotter measured 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved eastward across the Lower Mississippi Valley on Christmas Eve, reaching the Southeast states during the overnight hours. At the same time, a rather strong upper level disturbance moved southeast across the central Plains. This combination of systems resulted in widespread snowfall across western Kentucky. Average snowfall amounts were two to three inches. There was a swath of 4 to 5 inches that extended from west to east across the Kentucky Lake and Pennyrile regions. The swath extended from eastern Carlisle County and northern Graves County, eastward across the Benton and Eddyville areas, to around Princeton and Greenville. Specific snowfall reports included 5 inches in Greenville, 4 inches at Dawson Springs and Calvert City, 3.5 inches at Hopkinsville and Paducah, and 3 inches at Morganfield. Much of the snow fell in a three-hour window, when visibility dropped to one-half mile and accumulations neared an inch per hour. Roads became slick and hazardous, causing vehicles to run off the road. Numerous accidents were reported. Accidents with injuries occurred in Henderson County, including one on the Audubon Parkway. In Christian County, one person was injured in an accident. Several vehicles became stuck on hills in Muhlenberg County.

On Tuesday November 30th, a strong low pressure system with a abundant moisture and strong winds moved into the TN and OH valleys. The drenching rains caused the major rivers in the area to rise to above flood levels. This prompted Flood warnings across the area that lasted into December the 2nd. The front also had strong synoptic winds that prompted the need for a wind advisory on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Zion Road was underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of November 16th, a low pressure system continued to intensify and deepen as it traveled northeast along the windward side of the Appalachians. The associated cold front produced strong nonconvective winds and a Lake Wind Advisory needed to be issued. There was also enough instability for a thunderstorm to develop that was able to produce severe criteria winds. The winds did not subside till shortly after sunset. According to the south Kentucky KECC, hundreds of people were without power in Pulask, McCreary, and Wayne counties, due mostly to fallen trees and limbs during the afternoon and evening hours of November 16th.

Specific snowfall reports included 5 inches in Greenville, 4 inches at Dawson Springs and Calvert City, 3.5 inches at Hopkinsville and Paducah, and 3 inches at Morganfield. Much of the snow fell in a three-hour window, when visibility dropped to one-half mile and accumulations neared an inch per hour. Roads became slick and hazardous, causing vehicles to run off the road. Numerous accidents were reported. Accidents with injuries occurred in Henderson County, including one on the Audubon Parkway. In Christian County, one person was injured in an accident. Several vehicles became stuck on hills in Muhlenberg County.

During the day of November 16th, a low pressure system continued to intensify and deepen as it traveled northeast along the windward side of the Appalachians. The associated cold front produced strong nonconvective winds and a Lake Wind Advisory needed to be issued. There was also enough instability for a thunderstorm to develop that was able to produce severe criteria winds. The winds did not subside till shortly after sunset. According to the south Kentucky KECC, hundreds of people were without power in Pulask, McCreary, and Wayne counties, due mostly to fallen trees and limbs during the afternoon and evening hours of November 16th.

On Saturday December 4th, a quick moving clipper system moved through the area. Some wintry precipitation fell creating slick and hazardous roadways. Most areas received 1 to 2 inches with larger amounts of 3 to 4 inches reported. A winter weather advisory was issued due to the snow and wintry precipitation. Afterward, a significant upslope event ensued resulting in higher accumulation totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The total snowfall was 2 inches.

On Tuesday November 30th, a strong low pressure system with a abundant moisture and strong winds moved into the TN and OH valleys. The drenching rains caused the major rivers in the area to rise to above flood levels. This prompted Flood warnings across the area that lasted into December the 2nd. The front also had strong synoptic winds that prompted the need for a wind advisory on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 dispatch reported that water is over the road at the intersection of Highways 6 and 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 16th, a low pressure system developed in the plains and then tracked east across the Tennessee Valley. The warm front moved just south of the forecast area. Wintry precipitation fell as freezing rain from the Tennessee border and north up to the Mountain Parkway. The northern counties such as Fleming, Bath, and Rowan received all snow from the event. South of the Mountain Parkway, heavy ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch were common. This resulted in many homes being without power for an extended period of time. Perry county had more than 2000 residents without power. Breathitt county had 200 customers without power. Leslie county had nearly 4000 customers without power. The northern counties reported 4 to 6 inches of snow from the storm. The surface still had a persistent cold layer and freezing drizzle continued into the 17th. This prompted the issuance of a new winter weather advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was falling as a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain at time of report. Three inches of snow fell in Phelps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snow storm of the 2010-11 winter season dumped between 2 and 12 inches of snow on eastern Kentucky on December 12th and 13th. The highest snowfall totals occurred in Bell, Lee, and Leslie, and McCreary counties where 10-12 inches were reported. There were several culprits for this snow event, as areas of low pressure moving across the lower Great Lakes and up the eastern slopes of the Appalachian Mountains combined forces over the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. The highest snowfall totals occurred along the Kentucky River basin as an intense band of moderate to heavy snowfall remained in place there during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported a 12 hour snowfall total of 6.5 inches near Talbert through 550 am. The total snowfall since 1 am on the 13th was 4.2 inches.
